coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of ultrathin carbon nanosheets through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from carbon quantum dots as lithium-ion batteries anodes
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Feng
Xing, Baolin
Zeng, Huihui
Guo, Hui
Qu, Xiaoxiao
Huang, Guangxu
Cao, Yijun
Li, Peng
Feng, Laihong
Zhang, Chuanxiang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanosheets (CNSs) have drawn numerous attention as promising anodes in energy storage devices due to their large specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity close to graphene materials. Restricted by the expensive raw materials, harsh conditions and cumbersome post-processing procedures, the large-scale production of CNSs is a major challenge. Herein, ultrathin CNSs were constructed through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from coal tar pitch-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The ice can manipulate the salt crystal growth direction and dominantly guide the distribution of CQDs on the surface of NaCl-KCl crystal templates, whereas the liquid salts not only act as the solvent to load and disperse CQDs but as molecular templates to facilitate the formation of ultrathin CNSs during the carbonization process. The prepared CNSs exhibit twisted and ultrathin lamellar structures with a thickness of ∼1.70 nm, abundant hierarchical nanopores, and a moderate specific surface area of 302.00 m2·g−1. Benefiting from these unique microstructure features, the optimized CNS-3 adopted as anode for lithium-ion batteries displays prominent electrochemical features with high reversible capacity (453 mAh·g−1 at 50 mA·g−1), outstanding rate capability (225 mAh·g−1 at 1000 mA·g−1), and superior cycle stability (94.3% capacitance retention after 500 cycles at 500 mA·g−1). Our work provides a novel and effective approach to fabricate ultrathin carbon nanosheets by employing low-cost carbon quantum dots derived from coal tar pitch as potential candidates applied for lithium-ion batteries and next-generation energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanosheets
Carbon quantum dots
Salt templates
Anode materials
Lithium-ion battery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52274261 , 51974110 and 52074109 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province ( 222300420167 ), the Research Fund of Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion ( CGCF202201 ), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province ( 21HASTIT008 ), the Key Scientific Research Project in Colleges and Universities of Henan Province ( 22A430022 ), and the Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province ( 212102310564 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the fabrication of CNSs by NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy.

SEM, TEM and AFM images of samples: SEM images of CNS-1 (a-b), CNS-2 (c-d), CNS-3 (e-f), and CNS-4 (g-h); (i) EDS images of C and O based on image e and TEM images (j-l) of CNS-3; AFM images of CNS-1 (m-n), CNS-2 (o-p), CNS-3 (q-r), and CNS-4 (s-t).
(a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) FT-IR, (d) XPS patterns and (e-f) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s of CNSs.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of the CNSs.

Electrochemical behaviors: (a) The first GCD curves of CNSs; (b) GCD curves in the first three cycles of CNS-3; (c) Rate capabilities of CNSs from 50 to 5000 mA·g−1; (d) Cycling performance and Coulombic efficiency of CNSs at 100 mA·g−1 for 200 cycles; (e) Cycling performance and Coulombic efficiency of CNS-3 at 500 and 5000 mA·g−1 for 1000 cycles, (f) CV curves of CNS-3 at 0.2 mV·s−1.

CNS-3 anode: (a) CV curves with different sweep rates; (b) The b-value (where b-value is equal to fitted-line slope); (c) Capacitive-effect contributions (red area); (d) The percentages of capacitive-effect contributions and diffusion-controlled contributions; (e) EIS behavior after different cycles (inset: the whole equivalent circuit); (f) GITT profiles and Li-ions diffusion coefficients; (g) Digital photographs of the CNS-3 anode lighting LEDs.
Microstructure characteristics and electrical conductivity of CNSs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of ultrathin carbon nanosheets through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from carbon quantum dots as lithium-ion batteries anodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanosheets (CNSs) have drawn numerous attention as promising anodes in energy storage devices due to their large specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity close to graphene materials. Restricted by the expensive raw materials, harsh conditions and cumbersome post-processing procedures, the large-scale production of CNSs is a major challenge. Herein, ultrathin CNSs were constructed through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from coal tar pitch-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The ice can manipulate the salt crystal growth direction and dominantly guide the distribution of CQDs on the surface of NaCl-KCl crystal templates, whereas the liquid salts not only act as the solvent to load and disperse CQDs but as molecular templates to facilitate the formation of ultrathin CNSs during the carbonization process. The prepared CNSs exhibit twisted and ultrathin lamellar structures with a thickness of ∼1.70 nm, abundant hierarchical nanopores, and a moderate specific surface area of 302.00 m2·g−1. Benefiting from these unique microstructure features, the optimized CNS-3 adopted as anode for lithium-ion batteries displays prominent electrochemical features with high reversible capacity (453 mAh·g−1 at 50 mA·g−1), outstanding rate capability (225 mAh·g−1 at 1000 mA·g−1), and superior cycle stability (94.3% capacitance retention after 500 cycles at 500 mA·g−1). Our work provides a novel and effective approach to fabricate ultrathin carbon nanosheets by employing low-cost carbon quantum dots derived from coal tar pitch as potential candidates applied for lithium-ion batteries and next-generation energy storage devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ultrathin carbon nanosheets (CNSs) with large specific surface area and unique micro-meso-macropores structure are believed to guarantee the sufficient active sites and efficient channels for the Li-ions storage and diffusion. The assembled CNSs half-cells can keep red commercial light-emitting diodes (LEDs) operating for ∼30 min, demonstrating their promising prospect in practical use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) with high energy density, long cycle life, environmental friendliness, and no memory effect offer new opportunities for the next generation of rechargeable batteries to meet the rapid growth in portable electronic devices [1–3]. Graphite is the most extensively utilized anode material in commercial LIBs, but its low theoretical capacity (372 mAh·g−1) limits its further improvement in the battery performance [4–6]. Therefore, the pursuit of high-performance and economical anode materials is crucial for the advancement of next-generation LIBs technology.

Carbon nanosheets (CNSs) are a typical two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanomaterial with a graphene-like structure, which are widely applied in energy-related and other fields [7–10], due to the fact that the ultrathin nanosheets have the advantages of shortening the electron transfer distance and promoting electrolyte penetration [11]. Typical approaches for the production of CNSs include applying porous silica [12], layered metal oxides [13–15], and 2D layered minerals (e.g., montmorillonite [16–18], vermiculite [19,20]) as templates in the carbonization of carbon-containing precursors. However, as far as the above-mentioned templates are concerned, a complex and environmentally harmful process (e.g., the use of strong alkalis and concentrated acids) is usually required to remove these templates, which can be problematic for large-scale production. Additionally, most reported CNSs have either a considerable thickness (18–100 nm) [21,22] or a relatively limited specific surface area (50–100 m2·g−1) [23]. This is not conducive to fast charge transfer and the penetration of electrolyte during the lithiation/delithiation reaction. Therefore, in the absence of scalable and well-controlled production methods, CNSs cannot fully realize their potential in energy conversion and storage applications.

In line with the search for alternative templates, molten salts, such as NaCl [24,25], KCl [26], and Na2SO4 [27], have a typical crystal structure with regular grain shapes and their outer surfaces can provide ideal substrates as structural templates. In addition, the mixed salt system has a lower melting point compared to the single salt [28]. During annealing, the salt is effectively embedded in the carbon material skeleton, which facilitates the rapid evolution and controllable formation of its microstructure. With the help of a few mixed salts (e.g., LiCl-KCl [29], KCl-ZnCl2 [30], and ZnCl2-FeCl3 [31] system), CNSs with different thicknesses have been successfully prepared. Despite these advancements, the above studies have more or less drawbacks. On one hand, among all mixed salt systems, the NaCl-KCl system is reported to be more superior because of its high electrical conductivity, low melting point and low cost [32,33]. On the other hand, in terms of cost, coal-based carbon sources may be the best option compared to other expensive and unsustainable carbon sources. CQDs with tiny size (<10 nm) are an emerging star in the carbon nanomaterials family [34,35]. They mainly consist of amorphous-to-nanocrystalline cores and an abundance of numerous O-containing functional groups on the surface. The O-containing functional groups have a positive effect on the subsequent functionalization of the modifications. Thus, CQDs can be easily manipulated to precisely control the structure of the desired product. However, at present, CQDs are often used as active additives in combination with other materials (e.g., various carbon materials, conductive polymers, and metal oxides) to improve electrochemical properties. Additionally, there is little information in the open literature on the construction of CNSs from CQDs by self-assembly or induced assembly strategies and using them as electrode materials.

In this work, ultrathin CNSs are produced through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy using low-cost CQDs from coal tar pitch. The influence of NaCl-KCl salt dosages on the microstructure and surface chemistry features of CNSs is analyzed and investigated in depth; while the formation mechanism of the obtained CNSs is further explored and elucidated based on the characterization method. Besides, the Li-ions storage performances of CNSs have exhibited prominent electrochemical performance.
Hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl) and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Inc.

Ultrathin CNSs were fabricated through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from low-cost CQDs. First, the CQDs were prepared in our laboratory based on the previous study [36]. Then, 5 g of NaCl and KCl (mass ratio 1:1) mixture was dissolved in CQDs aqueous solution (100 mL, CQDs at 5 mg·mL−1). Thereafter, the above prepared solution was immediately freeze-dried for 48 h and a white loose powder was collected. Subsequently, the powder was heated to 800 °C at 2 °C min−1 under the protection of nitrogen and kept for 2 h. Finally, the above products were repeatedly washed using distilled water and ethanol and dried at 80 °C. The final dried samples were noted as CNS-1, CNS-2, CNS-3, and CNS-4 (mixed salt masses of 5, 10, 15, and 20 g, respectively). For comparison, the samples without salt or ice were also synthesized. It is noted that without ice means that the solution was stoving drying at 80 °C.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Merlin Compact), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) were used to observe the morphology and nanostructures of CNSs, respectively. The structures and components of as-prepared samples were evaluated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) on a diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 40 kV in a scanning range of 5–80 °, Raman spectra (Raman, inVia, Renishaw, UK) with a 532 nm wavelength and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA). The functional group or chemical bond of carbon materials was examined by the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, Bruker Tensor 27). Nitrogen adsorption and desorption experiments were measured on a Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ-MP automatic physical adsorption instrument at − 196 °C. All samples were degassed at 200 °C for 10 h before the nitrogen adsorption measurement. The specific surface areas were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method from the adsorption data in the relative pressure (P/P0) ranging from 0.01 to 0.99, and the pore size distribution was evaluated by the density functional theory (DFT) model. The electrical conductivity was measured with a Nanometrics HL5550 cryostat by means of the standard four-probe technique at the pressure of 10 MPa.

The electrode sheets were prepared as follows. CNSs powder, carbon black and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in the mass ratio of 8:1:1 were ground and added to N-menthyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) to form a homogeneous paste. Subsequently, the slurry was pasted onto a copper foil and dried in vacuum oven at 80 °C for 10 h. The corresponding mass loading of active material on working electrode with a diameter of 14 mm was around 0.7–1.0 mg·cm−2. CR2016 half-cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box with less than 0.1 ppm of H2O and O2. Pure lithium foil, polypropylene membrane (Celgard 2400) and 1 M of LiPF6 solution (EC: DEC, 1:1, v/v), were selected as the counter electrode, separator, and electrolyte, respectively. Adopting the Neware battery testing system to record the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) data. The test voltage window was 0.01–3.0 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E).

The general processing route of ultrathin CNSs is schematically shown in Fig. 1. The mixed salts were dissolved in CQDs aqueous solution. It is soluble and can ionize cations and anions without affecting the stability of CQDs in the solution. Salt crystals were formed in-suit at about − 40 °C during the freeze-drying process, and their growth direction was controlled by ice. Meanwhile, the ice also dominates the distribution of CQDs on the surface of the salt crystals. White loose powder was obtained after freeze-drying as shown in Fig. S1. In the subsequent carbonization process, the salts begin to melt when the temperature approaches the salts' melting point (658 °C) [28]. The liquid phase molten salts then became the solvent for loading and dispersing the CQDs to avoid their aggregation and fusion, which favors the construction of carbon nanosheets. As the temperature rises to 800 °C, the high energy Cl- could diffuse and etch the carbon networks, which is conducive to the formation of pores. After natural cooling to ambient temperature, a distinct boundary between the salts and carbon materials could be seen (Fig. S2). This phenomenon was caused by the different polarities of the salts and carbon. Finally, the final product is purified, and the used salts can be recycled. This represents a substantial advancement in the creation of green and cost-effective synthesis routes.

SEM and TEM were adopted to observe the microscopic morphologies of CNSs. The CNSs show well-defined 2D sheet structures, which is depicted in Fig. 2a-h. It is found that the thickness of CNSs decreases with increasing salt content, due to the creation of more crystal surfaces for loading and dispersing CQDs, thus providing a stronger confinement effect, which will be further verified later. In particular, the CNS-3 is ultrathin, and the wrinkles are rich (Fig. 2e-f), which leads to rapid diffusion of electrolyte ions. As the amount of salt further increases accompanied by stronger Cl- etching, the nanosheets of CNS-4 are almost damaged (Fig. 2g-h). Moreover, Fig. 2i shows that CNS-3 contains C and O elements which are uniformly distributed. Samples without salt or ice were also synthesized to investigate the impact of ice and salt on the formation of ultrathin 2D structures (Fig. S3). In the absence of salt, the prepared samples appeared to have a 3D carbon framework morphology composed of the accumulation of carbon nanosheets. However, in the absence of ice, similar carbon nanosheet morphologies were observed. It is evident that the carbon nanosheets are very thick compared to CNSs. The TEM (Fig. 2j-k) images show that CNS-3 consists of many loose thin nanosheets that are interconnected and stacked. The nanosheets are almost transparent, which reveals their ultrathin properties. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2l indicates that graphite stripes are preserved in CNS-3. The graphite stripes are not infinitely connected to form well closed shells, revealing that each layer contains some defects that could form abundant nanoscale channels and voids, and the micropores can be well connected by these channels. Based on the AFM analysis (Fig. 2m-t), the thicknesses of CNS-1, CNS-2, CNS-3 and CNS-4 are ∼3.62 nm, ∼2.87 nm, ∼1.70 nm and ∼1.38 nm, which are thinner than most of the 2D materials previously reported in the literature (5 nm for HAS-PCNs-PACP [37], 6 nm for G-CTF-15 [38], 4.16 nm for CNS/CF0.15 [39]). It is evidently that the microscopic morphologies of CNSs are well tailorable in thickness and curvature by varying the salt dosages. The construction of ultrathin structures may be associated with the synergistic action of ice and salt. In the freeze-drying stage, the ice template manipulates the growth direction of the salt crystals and dominantly guides the distribution of CQDs on the salt crystals surface. During carbonization, the strongly polarized molten salt causes the CQDs to “dissolve” in the liquid molten salt. At this point, the liquid salt provides an in-built template for the growth of the carbon nanosheets. Under high temperature (800 °C) conditions, the resulting carbon fragments undergo coupling and rearrangement in the presence of salt ions, eventually resulting in graphene-like carbon nanosheets. This ultrathin structure results in a shortened ion transport path, an enlarged interface contact area of electrode/electrolyte, and increased adsorption sites for Li-ions storage, which promises to achieve satisfactory electrochemical properties.

The XRD patterns of the composites before carbonization after freeze-drying are shown in Fig. S4. All the diffraction peaks of the four composites can be exclusively attributed to NaCl (PDF#05-0628) and KCl (PDF#41-1476). No representative CQDs diffraction signals are observed in the composites, which is probably related to the small quantity and low crystalline of CQDs. This phenomenon is consistent with our previous report [40,41]. Fig. 3a illustrates the XRD patterns of the acquired CNSs. Broad and weak peaks at ∼22° and ∼43°, labelled as the (002) and (100) crystal planes of amorphous carbon, are observed in all patterns [42,43]. Meanwhile, no additional peaks appeared in any of the four samples, which suggests that sodium/potassium-containing compounds have been eliminated from CNSs. Furthermore, the (002) peaks shift to the left as the amount of salt template increases, indicating the increase in the amount of amorphous carbon and a gradual increase of d002 (interlayer distance) in CNSs [44]. As shown in Table 1, the d002 values are ranked as follows: CNS-4 (0.358 nm) > CNS-3 (0.356 nm) > CNS-2 (0.353 nm) > CNS-1 (0.346 nm) > graphite (0.335 nm). The enlarged carbon layer spacing facilitates the transfer of electrons and Li-ions. Fig. 3b shows two typical peaks in their Raman spectra, which correspond to peak D (disordered or defective graphite band) at ∼1350 cm−1, and peak G (crystalline graphite band) at∼1580 cm−1 [45]. In addition, the degree of defects in carbon materials is qualitatively assessed using ID/IG, which is the intensity ratio of the D peak to the G peak. The ID/IG fitting results are 0.84, 0.86, 0.87, and 0.89 for CNS-1, CNS-2, CNS-3, and CNS-4, respectively. As the amount of salt increases, the crystallinity of CNSs decreases and the disorder increases. This may be due to the fact that a large amount of salt templates would disperse the CQDs and introduce numerous defects, which in turn reduces the crystallinity of the final products. As mentioned above, d002 and ID/IG increase with increasing amounts of salt. This suggests that the molten salt may influence the generation of graphitic carbon, in accordance with the XRD results. It is widely accepted that ion charge transport is strongly associated with some disordered and defective structures. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c) reveal characteristic peaks of C-O-C (∼1100 cm−1), CC (∼1593 and ∼1410 cm−1), and -OH (∼3426 cm−1), indicating that a certain amount of oxygen content is still retained in CNSs [34]. The strong C1s peak located at ∼284.5 eV and weak O1s peak at ∼532.3 eV further indicate the existence of O-containing functional groups. The O1s peaks may be from the precursor CQDs [34]. Fig. 3e-f show that the fitted high-resolution C 1 s spectra of CNSs are divided into three peaks at ∼ 284.6 eV, ∼ 286.5 eV and ∼ 288.2 eV, which are assigned to CC, C-O and CO, respectively [34]. This agrees with the FT-IR results. The precursor CQDs have strong oxidation characteristics and abundant O-containing functional groups. Most of them are removed during carbonization at 800 °C, and only small quantities of C-O-C and -CO are retained. Notably, the coexistence of O-containing functional groups in the CNSs can promote the wettability of carbon material, which will ensure a fast transfer rate of electrolyte ions in nanopores and high available surface area. Moreover, they may increase the surface defects, resulting in the introduction of more electrochemical active sites for Li-ions storage to achieve a high capacity [25]. Table S1 lists the CNSs components measured by XPS. The porous structure of CNSs was characterized and tested by means of N2 adsorption-desorption instrument. The four isotherms are classified by IUPAC as type IV curves with distinct hysteresis loops, as depicted in Fig. 4a [46,47]. This indicates that the four materials contain many mesopores. The details of the specific surface area and pore structure parameters of CNSs are summarized in Table 1. The specific surface area of CNSs increases from 129.69 m2·g−1 for CNS-1 to 334.42 m2·g−1 for CNS-2 with increasing salt dosages, which indicates that salt has a positive effect on the increase in specific surface area. Fig. 4b confirms the micro-meso-macropores hierarchical structure of CNSs, where micropores are centered at ∼1.5 nm, pronounced mesopores are centered at ∼15 nm, and macropores are centered at ∼85 nm. It is widely accepted that gases released from carbon precursors during carbonization are the main cause of micropores. The high-energy Cl- from the molten salt template then promotes the formation of mesopores and macropores in CNSs. Accumulation of electrolyte ions occurs primarily in micropores of carbon materials, the mesopores can accelerate mass transfer and improve effective utilization of the pore surface area, and macropores can act as ion-buffering reservoirs to minimize the ion diffusion distance. So, the micropores of CNSs are closely related with the specific capacity, mesopores significantly influence the rate performance as well as specific capacity. Such hierarchical porous carbon nanosheets with interconnected macropores, well-defined mesopores and abundant micropores will provide sufficient available space for the storage and propagation of Li-ions, as a result, improving the energy storage capacity of CNSs. However, in CNS-4, the interconnection of the CQDs may not be complete due to excessive templates, resulting in a relatively weak carbon skeleton. Thus, some of the micropores and mesopores may merge during carbonization, leading to a decrease in pore volume. The above results strongly confirm that regulating salt template ratio can effectively adjust the porosity of the CNSs. The CNS-3 is promising to yield excellent electrochemical performance because of its suitable specific surface area, micro-meso-macropores hierarchical structure, and significantly enhanced electrical conductivity (see Table 1), which allows for more efficient Li-ions transport.

Fig. 5a shows the first GCD curves for the CNSs anodes at 50 mA·g−1. The initial charge/discharge capacities of the CNS-1, CNS-2, CNS-3, and CNS-4 anodes are 424/799 mAh·g−1, 406/833 mAh·g−1, 504/987 mAh·g−1, and 438/829 mAh·g−1, with the relative Coulombic efficiencies of 53.1%, 48.7%, 51.1%, and 52.8%, respectively. As the salt dosages increase, the initial Li-ions storage capacities first increase and then decrease. In contrast, the CNS-3 anode has the highest capacity. Combined with microstructure characteristics analysis of the former part, the splendid Li-ions storage capacity of CNS-3 is probably due to the balance between specific surface area, surface oxygen content, and electrical conductivity. To learn more about the Li-ions storage performances of the CNS-3 anode, Fig. 5b further displays its GCD curves for the first three cycles. In the first discharge curve, a discharge plateau is observed at approximately 0.75–0.9 V. It is generally accepted that this plateau implies the formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) films. In the later cycles, the plateau almost disappears, and the charge/discharge curves almost overlap. The second charge/discharge capacities are 465/506 mAh·g−1 and the third cycle are 455/482 mAh·g−1, respectively. The capacity tends to decrease compared to the capacity of the starting cycle. The irreversible capacity loss is explained by the formation of SEI films and the reaction of Li-ions with some structural defects, resulting in the irreversible insertion of Li-ions inside the carbon matrix [48,49]. In addition, the corresponding Coulombic efficiencies improves to 91.9% and 94.4% respectively. Fig. S5 shows the GCD curves in the initial three cycles of CNS-1, CNS-2, and CNS-4. The similar GCD behaviors of the four anodes indicate that a stable redox reaction has largely developed after the first cycle. Fig. 5c shows the rate capabilities of the four anodes at 50–5000 mA·g−1. The CNS-3 anode has the highest reversible capacity of the four anodes within the trial current density range, indicating a prominent rate performance. After 10 cycles at 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, and 5000 mA·g−1, the average reversible capacity of CNS-3 reaches 453, 351, 299, 256, 225, 189, and 142 mAh·g−1 at the respective current density. After 70 cycles as described above, the reversible capacity of CNS-3 is as high as 415 mAh·g−1 at 50 mA·g−1, demonstrating its favorable reversibility and structural stability. The reversible capacities of CNS-1, CNS-2, and CNS-4 under the same test conditions are 253, 369, and 348 mAh·g−1, respectively. Fig. 5d compares cycle capabilities of CNSs anodes at 100 mA·g−1 for 200 cycles. CNSs exert outstanding cycling stability, in which the average reversible capacity of CNS-3 is up to 468 mAh·g−1, significantly higher than that of CNS-1 (315 mAh·g−1), CNS-2 (413 mAh·g−1) and CNS-4 (334 mAh·g−1). Furthermore, the cycling stability of CNS-3 is substantially better than reported nanostructured carbons, such as porous carbon [50], mesoporous nanosheets [51,52], and even some composites, for instance, silicon/carbon composites G@Si@C composite [53] and Sn/carbon composite [54]. Fig. 5e exhibits the charge/discharge specific capacities of CNS-3 anode for 500 cycles at 500 and 5000 mA·g−1, respectively. At 500 mA·g−1, the capacity of CNS-3 decreases significantly in the first few cycles, reaching about 387 mAh·g−1 in the subsequent 300 cycles, and about 365 mAh·g−1 in the last 200 cycles. After the above 500 cycles, the capacity retention rate is up to 94.3%. In addition, the Coulombic efficiency of the CNS-3 anode is close to 99.6% in the subsequent cycles, except for the first several cycles. Furthermore, the long-term electrochemical stability of the CNS-3 anode is evaluated. After 500 cycles at the lower current density (500 mA·g−1) described above, the current density is set to 5000 mA·g−1 and the CNS-3 anode is cycled continuously for another 500 cycles. As expected, CNS-3 anode exhibits prominent cycling stability. This can be proven by the reversible capacity is up to ∼157 mAh·g−1 and the Coulombic efficiency is nearly 99.8%. The excellent electrochemical properties give CNS-3 a promising future for practical applications in LIBs.

Fig. 5 f and Fig. S6 show the CV curves for CNSs from 0.01 to 3.0 V, taking CNS-3 as an example for detailed analysis. For the first discharge process, the CNS-3 anode exhibits a distinct peak ∼1.4 V and a weak peak ∼0.3 V, which correspond to the process of Li-ions intercalation into the micropores and the formation of SEI film [5]. In the initial charge cycle, a weak peak ∼1.1 V is associated with the extraction of Li-ions from the carbon nanosheets and nanopores [55]. Furthermore, the CV curves of the second and third cycles overlap almost exactly after the first cycle. This can be explained by the fact that a stable lithiation/delithiation reaction has been formed in the CNS-3 anode with good reversibility.

Kinetic analysis based on the CV curves is an efﬁcient means to study the storage mechanism of Li-ions. Fig. 6a displays the CV curves of CNS-3 at 0.1–0.9 mV·s−1. With the increase of scanning rate, the shape of CV curve is maintained well, indicating small polarization and high stability of the corresponding anodes. Fig. 6b reveals the linear relationship between the peak current (i) and the scanning rate (v). According to the expression i = av b, the slope b of CNS-3 at the cathode peak and anode peak is 0.86 and 0.91, respectively [56,57]. This indicates that there are diffusion-controlled and capacitive-effect processes in Li-ions storage behavior of CNS-3 anode. Then the sweep rate is set to 0.5 mV·s−1, and a 65% contribution of the capacitive-effect is calculated (Fig. 6c). Moreover, the capacitive contribution is increasing as the sweep speed increases from 0.1 to 0.9 mV·s−1 (Fig. 6d). For instance, the capacitive contribution reaches 76% at 0.9 mV·s−1. Fig. S7 shows the capacitance contributions of CNS-1, CNS-2, and CNS-4 under the same test conditions. CNSs have abundant O-containing functional groups and well-developed mesoporous structures which help boost capacitive energy storage. Fig. 6e shows the EIS plots before and after cycling of the CNS-3 anode and the equivalent circuit model diagram utilized for impedance fitting. Rs indicates internal resistance, RSEI denotes SEI films resistance, and Rct stand for charge-transfer resistance, respectively [58]. It is found that the fitted Rct value of the CNS-3 anode decreases from an initial value of 196 Ω to 109 Ω after 3 cycles. After 200 cycles, the value decreases to 52 Ω. This indicates that the reaction kinetics of the CNS-3 anode improved after 3 cycles and remained stable in the following cycles. Furthermore, the GITT test is utilized to quantify the diffusion coefficient (D) of Li-ions for the CNS-3 anode. Fig. 6f shows the typical curves of CNS-3 obtained by the GITT. The lithiation/delithiation process is fluctuating and shows a decreasing trend, which is used to determine the DLi +. The initial Li-ions intercalation may have prevented contact with the deeper active sites, which leads to a limited amount of Li-ions diffusion in the anode. The DLi + values obtained for the CNS-3 anode ranged between 10−9 and 10−10.5 cm2·s−1, illustrating its excellent Li-ions storage behavior [59,60]. More interestingly, Fig. 6g shows that CNSs half-cells can keep red commercial light-emitting diodes (LEDs) operating for ∼30 min, demonstrating its promising prospect in practical use. CNSs were also successfully prepared using the KCl-ZnCl2 and LiCl-KCl (Fig. S8) systems. As described above, we believe that the remarkable electrochemical properties of CNSs demonstrate the potential of the universal method for energy storage devices. To study the structure changes during long cycling, the structure of CNS-3 after 200 cycles at 100 mA·g−1 is investigated using SEM and TEM. The disassembled electrodes are dipped in dimethyl carbonate (DMC) for a day and subsequently rinsed with ethanol and deionized water. The sheet morphology is similar that before cycling, and no structural deformation or severe aggregation is observed after cycles for the CNS-3 anode (Fig. S9a). The EDS elemental mappings detects a distribution of P and F elements, corresponding to the composition of the SEI film covering the material surface of the LiPF6 salt-based electrolyte system (Fig. S9b) [36]. In Fig. S9c-d, the sheet morphology still can be clearly observed but the lattice fringes are not as clear as before cycling due to the formation of SEI film. The above observations manifest that the Li-ions insertion/extraction in CNS-3 anode is reversible without structural degradation and pulverization, thus stable structure results in excellent electrochemical performances.

In summary, we developed a facile and simple method to fabricate ultrathin carbon nanosheets through NaCl-KCl templates coupled with ice-induced assembly strategy from coal tar pitch-based carbon quantum dots. The obtained CNS-3 has a stack thickness of ∼1.70 nm, which facilitates the shortening of electron transport distance and promotes electrolyte penetration. Furthermore, the CNS-3 has a moderate specific surface area (302.00 m2·g−1), a unique micro-meso-macropores structure, and excellent conductivity (128.9 S·cm−1). Given these merits, the CNS-3 as anodes displays remarkable Li-ions storage performances. Specifically, it delivers a higher first discharge capacity of 987 mAh·g−1 at 50 mA·g−1, a superb rate capability of 225 mAh·g−1 at 1000 mA·g−1, and long-term cycle stability during 1000 cycles. We believe that our current work provides a universal approach for preparing potential candidate anodes for high-performance LIBs to meet the growing demand for applications.

Xing Baolin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shi Feng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Guo Hui: Supervision, Validation. Zeng Huihui: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Qu Xiaoxiao: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Cao Yijun: Resources, Supervision. Huang Guangxu: Supervision. Feng Laihong: Supervision. Li Peng: Resources, Supervision. Zhang Chuanxiang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52274261, 51974110 and 52074109), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (222300420167), the Research Fund of Henan Key Laboratory of Coal Green Conversion (CGCF202201), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (21HASTIT008), the Key Scientific Research Project in Colleges and Universities of Henan Province (22A430022), and the Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (212102310564).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.173325.
